name: Cat-Cow Flow
purpose: Warms spinal column segment by segment.
whyForYou: Post-surgical back needs gentle segmental motion before any load.
steps: All fours — hands under shoulders, knees under hips.
COW: Inhale 3s — drop belly, lift chest and tailbone.
CAT: Exhale 3s — round spine, tuck chin.
Move SLOWLY — one breath per transition.
commonMistakes: ❌ Only moving head/hips instead of full spine
coreBracing: LIGHT (20%) — gentle abdominal engagement, no brace.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: Sharp pain in cow = reduce range. Move within pain-free arc only.
⚠️ KNEE: Pad knees if kneeling is uncomfortable.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Wrist pain = make fists or use dumbbells as handles.
proTip: Close eyes — feel segmental motion better.
contraindications: Acute disc herniation with flexion intolerance

name: Dead Bug
purpose: Anti-extension — most important pattern for protecting surgical site.
whyForYou: Retrains deconditioned deep stabilizers without spinal load.
steps: Lie flat. Press lower back INTO floor — no gap.
Arms straight up. Knees bent to 90°.
SLOWLY extend RIGHT arm + LEFT leg. Back stays GLUED to floor.
Exhale 4s during extension. Pause 1s. Return. Switch sides.
coreBracing: MODERATE (40-50%) — press back into floor, maintain throughout.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: If lower back lifts off floor, BEND the extending leg instead of straightening it.
⚠️ KNEE: No impact — knees stay at 90°.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Keep arms within comfortable range.
proTip: Place a towel under lower back — if it slides out, you lost the brace.
contraindications: Acute lower back flare-up (switch to breathing drills only)

name: Banded External Rotation
purpose: Pre-activates rotator cuff before any pressing movement.
whyForYou: Left labrum tear requires RC activation before loading shoulder.
steps: Pin elbow to side at 90° bend.
Rotate forearm outward approximately 45°.
Return SLOWLY under control. Always start with LEFT side.
commonMistakes: ❌ Band too heavy — should be easy
injuryNotes: ⚠️ SHOULDER: MANDATORY before any pressing. Front pinch = reduce range. This exercise HELPS the labrum.
⚠️ BACK: Stand tall — no leaning.
⚠️ KNEE: No impact.
proTip: Pin a rolled towel between elbow and ribs for perfect form feedback.

name: Glute Bridge
purpose: Activates glutes. When glutes are dormant, the back compensates.
whyForYou: Post-surgical back = inhibited glutes. Must wake them up first.
steps: Lie on back, knees bent, feet hip-width apart.
SQUEEZE glutes first — then push through HEELS.
Rise until body forms straight line from knees to shoulders. HOLD 1s.
Lower slowly — 2s descent.
commonMistakes: ❌ Feeling it in lower back instead of glutes = STOP
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: If you feel this in your back instead of glutes, STOP. Reduce height. Squeeze glutes harder before lifting.
⚠️ KNEE: Feet position affects knee stress — adjust until comfortable.
⚠️ SHOULDER: No impact.
proTip: Place a fist between your knees and squeeze — turbocharges glute activation.

name: VMO Wall Sit
purpose: Isolates VMO — the #1 patellar tracking protector.
whyForYou: Left VMO is weaker after knee surgery. Must pre-activate before squatting.
steps: Back flat against wall. Slide down to approximately 45° knee bend.
Focus on inner quad (VMO). Actively flex it.
Hold 20s. Breathe continuously — don't hold breath.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ KNEE: 45° MAX — never 90°. Joint pain = too deep, come up. VMO activation is mandatory before any squatting pattern.
⚠️ BACK: Keep back flat on wall — no arching.
⚠️ SHOULDER: No impact.
proTip: Place hand on inner quad (VMO) — feel it contract. If you can't feel it, you're too deep.

name: Bird Dog
purpose: McGill Big 3 — anti-rotation and anti-extension under contralateral load.
whyForYou: Part of your mandatory McGill Big 3 daily protocol for back rehab.
steps: All fours — hands under shoulders, knees under hips.
Brace core gently. Extend RIGHT arm and LEFT leg simultaneously.
Hold 1s at full extension — body forms straight line.
Return slowly. Complete all reps one side, then switch.
formCues: ✅ Thumb points up on extending arm
commonMistakes: ❌ Moving too fast — this is a stability drill
coreBracing: MODERATE (30-40%) — enough to prevent trunk rotation.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: McGill Big 3 — prescribed daily. If back fatigues, reduce reps not range. Stop if any radiating pain.
⚠️ KNEE: Pad knees if kneeling is uncomfortable.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Keep reaching arm below shoulder height if labrum is aggravated.
proTip: Balance a water bottle on your lower back — if it falls, you're rotating.
contraindications: Acute disc herniation with extension intolerance

name: Quadruped Hip Circles
purpose: Lubricates hip joint through full available ROM before loading.
whyForYou: Tight hips force your back to compensate — mobilize hips to protect spine.
steps: All fours position — hands under shoulders, knees under hips.
Lift RIGHT knee off ground slightly.
Draw large circles with the knee — 8 forward, 8 backward.
Switch to LEFT side. Keep torso completely still.
formCues: ✅ Torso stays STILL — movement only from hip
✅ Circles as large as pain-free ROM allows
commonMistakes: ❌ Circles too small — explore full range
coreBracing: LIGHT (20%) — just enough to stabilize torso.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: Keep torso rigid — all movement from hip socket only.
⚠️ KNEE: If kneeling hurts, fold mat for extra padding.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Wrist discomfort = use fists or dumbbells as handles.
proTip: Think of stirring a pot with your knee — smooth, continuous circles.

name: Side-Lying Thoracic Rotation
purpose: Restores thoracic rotation — stiff T-spine forces lumbar compensation.
whyForYou: Your lower back is post-surgical — thoracic mobility takes rotational demand off the lumbar spine.
steps: Lie on LEFT side. Knees stacked at 90°, arms extended forward.
INHALE 3s. Rotate RIGHT arm open toward the ceiling/floor behind you.
HOLD 1s at end range. EXHALE 4s returning to start.
Complete all reps, then switch sides.
formCues: ✅ Knees stay STACKED — don't let top knee lift
✅ Move from mid-back, not lower back
commonMistakes: ❌ Forcing range — let gravity do the work
coreBracing: MINIMAL (10%) — this is a mobility drill, stay relaxed.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: Excellent for you — takes rotational stress off lumbar. Keep knees stacked to lock out lumbar rotation.
⚠️ KNEE: Place pillow between knees if uncomfortable.
⚠️ SHOULDER: If left shoulder pinches at end range, reduce rotation depth.
proTip: Exhale into the stretch — you'll gain 10-15° more rotation each breath.

name: Ankle Mobility Rocks
purpose: Restores ankle dorsiflexion — limited ankles cause knee valgus and back compensation.
whyForYou: Better ankle mobility = safer squat mechanics = less knee and back stress.
steps: Half-kneeling position — front foot flat, rear knee on pad.
Rock front knee forward over toes, keeping heel DOWN.
Push knee as far forward as possible without heel lifting.
Rock back to start. Repeat 10 times, then switch legs.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ KNEE: Keep knee tracking straight over 2nd toe. Stop if joint pain (not muscle stretch).
⚠️ BACK: Use wall or rack for balance if needed — no twisting.
⚠️ SHOULDER: No impact.
proTip: Place tape line 4 inches from wall — try to touch knee to wall without heel lifting. Track progress weekly.
contraindications: Acute ankle sprain (wait until weight-bearing is pain-free)

name: Scapular Push-Up
purpose: Activates serratus anterior — critical for shoulder blade stability during pressing.
whyForYou: Labrum tear requires rock-solid scapular control before loading the shoulder.
steps: High plank position — arms straight, hands under shoulders.
WITHOUT bending elbows, let shoulder blades pinch together (retract).
Push the floor away — spread shoulder blades apart (protract).
Move ONLY at the shoulder blades. Arms stay locked.
commonMistakes: ❌ Bending elbows (this is NOT a push-up)
❌ Rushing — this is a control drill
injuryNotes: ⚠️ SHOULDER: Start on KNEES if plank position aggravates labrum. This exercise HELPS shoulder stability.
⚠️ BACK: If plank position stresses back, do from knees or standing against wall.
⚠️ KNEE: Knee version available — pad knees on mat.
proTip: Do against a wall first if plank is too challenging. Same movement, less load.

name: Trap Bar Deadlift
purpose: King compound lift. Trap bar reduces lumbar shear ~20% vs straight bar.
whyForYou: Only approved deadlift variation — stays within your back's tolerance zone.
steps: Step INSIDE the trap bar. Grab HIGH handles. Set back FLAT.
BRACE hard. Push the FLOOR away — don't pull the bar up.
Hips drive FORWARD to lockout. Stand tall. REBRACE before every rep.
formCues: ✅ Flat back — chest up, lats engaged
✅ Push floor away — leg drive, not back pull
commonMistakes: ❌ ROUNDING BACK — immediate stop if this happens
❌ Jerking the bar off the floor
coreBracing: MAXIMUM (90-100%) — full Valsalva brace, rebrace every rep.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: HIGH handles ONLY until capacity increases. NO conventional deadlift — trap bar only. Any tingling, numbness, or radiating pain = STOP IMMEDIATELY. Stay under 70% 1RM.
⚠️ KNEE: Watch for knee valgus. VMO activation pre-sets are mandatory before this lift.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Neutral grip is shoulder-safe — no concerns here.
proTip: Flat shoes or barefoot. Imagine pushing the earth away from you.
contraindications: Acute lower back pain or flare-up
No trap bar available (use DB Romanian Deadlift instead)
Back safety level RESTRICTED or STOP

name: Landmine Press
purpose: Safest pressing pattern for labrum tears — arcing path avoids end-range danger.
whyForYou: Your labrum tear prohibits overhead pressing at end-range. This is the safe alternative.
steps: Staggered stance. Hold barbell end at shoulder height.
BRACE core. Press UP and forward along the arc.
Protract shoulder blade at top (push through). Return slowly.
formCues: ✅ Protract at top for full serratus engagement
coreBracing: MOD-HIGH (60-70%) — brace to prevent rotation and back arch.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ SHOULDER: Your safest pressing option. Any click or pinch = reduce weight or range. External rotation warm-up MANDATORY before this exercise.
⚠️ BACK: Brace hard. Staggered stance reduces spinal load vs bilateral.
⚠️ KNEE: Staggered stance — ensure front knee tracks straight.
proTip: Think 'punching the ceiling at an angle' — not pressing straight up.

name: Bulgarian Split Squat
purpose: Single-leg strength. Corrects bilateral imbalances.
whyForYou: Forces each leg to work independently — exposes and corrects left knee weakness.
steps: Rear foot on bench, laces down. Front foot positioned far enough forward.
DESCEND straight down — 3s controlled descent.
DRIVE through front HEEL to stand. Keep torso upright.
formCues: ✅ Knee tracks over toes — no valgus
commonMistakes: ❌ Rear foot doing too much work
injuryNotes: ⚠️ KNEE: Start BODYWEIGHT ONLY on left side. Watch for valgus. Depth limited — don't go below comfortable range. VMO pre-activation mandatory.
⚠️ BACK: Upright torso minimizes spinal load. Hold rack for balance if needed.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Holding dumbbells is fine — no overhead component.
proTip: Hold a squat rack for balance until you own the pattern. Depth comes later.
contraindications: Acute knee swelling on working leg
Deep flexion intolerance on left knee

name: Chest-Supported Row
purpose: Back thickness with ZERO spinal loading — chest supported removes back stress.
whyForYou: Safest rowing variation for your post-surgical back. Bench does the stabilizing.
steps: Lie face down on incline bench. Arms hang straight down.
RETRACT shoulder blades first — then PULL elbows to hips.
SQUEEZE shoulder blades together 1s at top. LOWER slowly.
formCues: ✅ Retract THEN pull — blade squeeze initiates
commonMistakes: ❌ Pulling with biceps instead of back
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: SAFEST row for you — zero spinal load. Go-to back exercise.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Use NEUTRAL grip (palms facing each other) to protect labrum. Avoid pronated grip.
⚠️ KNEE: No impact.
proTip: Think 'elbows to back pockets' — cues the lats over the biceps.
contraindications: Rib injury (lying on bench may be painful)

name: Pallof Press
purpose: Anti-rotation — the most functional core pattern. Rehab disguised as strength.
whyForYou: Your back needs anti-rotation stability more than any crunching movement.
steps: Stand perpendicular to cable. Handle at chest.
BRACE. PRESS handle straight out from chest. DON'T rotate.
HOLD 2s at full arm extension. Return slowly to chest.
formCues: ✅ Hips and shoulders stay SQUARE to front
✅ Hold at full extension — resist the pull
✅ Feet hip-width apart for stable base
commonMistakes: ❌ Too heavy — form breaks before fatigue
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: One of the BEST exercises for your back. Anti-rotation trains exactly what your spine needs.
⚠️ KNEE: Stand with soft knees — not locked.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Keep arms at chest height — no overhead reaching.
proTip: Progress to half-kneeling for extra core demand. Then tall-kneeling for maximum.

name: Face Pulls
purpose: The most important shoulder corrective exercise. Counterbalances all pressing.
whyForYou: MANDATORY counterbalance for labrum tear. Do this every session you press.
steps: Cable at HIGH position. Grab rope with overhand grip.
Pull elbows BACK and APART — bring rope to face level.
SQUEEZE rear delts 1s — finish in 'double bicep pose' position. Return slowly.
formCues: ✅ Pull to FACE level — not chest
✅ Pull rope APART at end range
commonMistakes: ❌ Pulling to chest instead of face
❌ Not pulling rope apart at end
coreBracing: LIGHT (20%) — this is an isolation movement.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ SHOULDER: This exercise HELPS your labrum. Mandatory counterbalance to all pressing. Should feel good — never painful.
⚠️ BACK: Light weight — no spinal stress.
⚠️ KNEE: No impact.
proTip: Finish each rep in 'double bicep pose' — external rotation at the top maximizes rotator cuff benefit.

name: Dumbbell Floor Press
purpose: Pressing with limited shoulder ROM — floor stops elbows from going too deep.
whyForYou: Floor limits range of motion to protect your labrum. Safer than bench press.
steps: Lie on floor, knees bent. Hold dumbbells at chest height.
Press dumbbells up — neutral grip (palms facing each other).
Lower until elbows TOUCH floor — brief pause. Press back up.
formCues: ✅ Pause on floor — eliminates bounce/stretch reflex
coreBracing: MODERATE (40-50%) — maintain neutral spine on floor.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ SHOULDER: Floor limits ROM to protect labrum — that's the point. Use NEUTRAL grip only. External rotation warm-up mandatory before this exercise.
⚠️ BACK: Floor supports spine. Keep back flat — no excessive arch.
⚠️ KNEE: No impact.
proTip: The pause on the floor kills momentum — builds true pressing strength without relying on stretch reflex.

name: Incline Push-Up
equipment: Bench, box, or sturdy elevated surface
purpose: Bodyweight pressing progression that's scalable and joint-friendly.
whyForYou: Adjustable difficulty — higher incline = less load. Perfect for building pressing capacity safely.
steps: Hands on elevated surface, shoulder-width apart.
Body forms straight line from head to heels.
Lower chest toward surface — 2s descent. Brief pause.
Push back up — protract shoulder blades at top.
formCues: ✅ Protract at top for serratus activation
injuryNotes: ⚠️ SHOULDER: Higher incline = less shoulder stress. Start HIGH (counter height) and progress lower as tolerated. External rotation warm-up first.
⚠️ BACK: Keep core braced — no sagging. If back hurts, go to higher incline.
⚠️ KNEE: No impact.
proTip: Counter height → bench height → low box → floor. Progress the incline down over weeks, not days.

name: Single-Arm Landmine Press
purpose: Unilateral pressing with anti-rotation demand — pressing + core in one move.
whyForYou: Same shoulder-safe arc as bilateral landmine press, plus forces core anti-rotation.
steps: Half-kneeling position. Hold barbell end in one hand at shoulder.
BRACE core hard — don't let torso rotate.
Press up along the arc. Protract at top. Return slowly.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ SHOULDER: Same safe arc as bilateral landmine press. Start with LEFT arm (weaker side) first. Click or pinch = reduce weight.
⚠️ BACK: Half-kneeling reduces spinal load. Brace hard to prevent rotation.
⚠️ KNEE: Pad the kneeling knee. Front knee tracks straight.
proTip: Squeeze the glute of the kneeling leg — locks your pelvis and protects your back.
contraindications: Inability to maintain half-kneeling without pain

name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Row
purpose: Unilateral back development with bench support reducing spinal load.
whyForYou: Hand-and-knee support on bench takes stress off your back while building pulling strength.
steps: One hand and knee on bench. Other foot on floor. Back FLAT.
Let dumbbell hang straight down. Shoulder relaxed.
RETRACT shoulder blade. PULL elbow to hip — squeeze 1s at top.
Lower slowly — full stretch at bottom.
formCues: ✅ Pull to hip, not to chest
✅ Initiate with shoulder blade, not bicep
commonMistakes: ❌ Rotating torso to get weight up
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: Bench support reduces spinal load significantly. Keep back FLAT — any rounding = reduce weight. Stop if pain.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Neutral grip. Don't force full stretch at bottom if labrum is sensitive.
⚠️ KNEE: Pad the kneeling knee on bench.
proTip: Think 'start the lawn mower' — drive elbow back, not just pulling with the hand.
contraindications: Acute lower back flare-up (use chest-supported row instead)

name: Seated Cable Row
equipment: Cable row machine, V-bar or neutral grip handle
purpose: Horizontal pulling with controlled load — great for back thickness.
whyForYou: Seated position with pad support limits spinal load while training pulling pattern.
steps: Sit with chest against pad (if available). Feet on platform, knees slightly bent.
Grab handle with neutral grip. Sit tall — slight lean forward at start.
PULL handle to lower chest/upper abdomen — squeeze shoulder blades 1s.
Return slowly — controlled stretch forward without rounding back.
formCues: ✅ Squeeze shoulder blades at peak contraction
commonMistakes: ❌ Rounding lower back on the stretch
❌ Pulling with biceps — not engaging back
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: Minimal forward lean — torso stays near upright. DO NOT round lower back on the forward stretch. Use chest-pad if machine has one.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Neutral grip (V-bar) is safest for labrum. Avoid wide/pronated grip.
⚠️ KNEE: Slight knee bend — no locking out.
proTip: Imagine a tennis ball between your shoulder blades — squeeze it at peak contraction.
contraindications: Acute lower back pain (switch to chest-supported row)

name: Band Pull-Apart
purpose: Rear delt and scapular retraction — counterbalances all forward-arm activities.
whyForYou: Home/outdoor alternative to face pulls. Same shoulder-protective benefit, no cable needed.
steps: Hold band at shoulder height, arms straight out, shoulder-width grip.
Pull band APART — squeeze shoulder blades together.
HOLD 1s at full retraction. Return slowly to start.
formCues: ✅ Chest opens — don't just move arms
commonMistakes: ❌ Band too heavy — should be high-rep easy
injuryNotes: ⚠️ SHOULDER: Excellent for labrum — builds rear delt strength to balance joint. Light band only. Should never cause pinching.
⚠️ BACK: Stand tall — no leaning.
⚠️ KNEE: No impact.
proTip: Do 20 reps between every set of pressing — shoulders will thank you.

name: Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift
purpose: Hip hinge pattern with dumbbells — home alternative to trap bar deadlift.
whyForYou: Your go-to hinge for home workouts. Lighter load than trap bar but same movement pattern.
steps: Stand with dumbbells at thighs, feet hip-width. Slight knee bend.
HINGE at hips — push butt BACK. Dumbbells slide down thighs.
Lower until hamstring stretch (mid-shin for most). Back stays FLAT.
SQUEEZE glutes to stand. Drive hips forward.
formCues: ✅ Hinge from HIPS — not rounding back
✅ Slight knee bend — not a stiff-leg deadlift
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: LIGHTER than trap bar — start with very light DBs to groove the pattern. Back must stay FLAT. Any rounding = stop. No radiating symptoms.
⚠️ KNEE: Slight bend protects knee. Not a deep squat pattern.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Just holding DBs — no stress.
proTip: Imagine closing a car door with your butt — that's the hip hinge.
contraindications: Inability to hip hinge without rounding

name: Cable Pull-Through
purpose: Teaches hip hinge pattern with constant cable tension — excellent glute builder.
whyForYou: Low-impact hinge that reinforces the pattern without heavy spinal loading.
steps: Face AWAY from low cable. Rope between legs. Step forward.
HINGE at hips — let cable pull you back. Soft knees.
DRIVE hips forward — squeeze glutes HARD at top. Stand tall.
formCues: ✅ Arms are just hooks — glutes do the work
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: Much lighter spinal load than deadlift. Excellent hinge teacher. Keep neutral spine.
⚠️ KNEE: Soft knee bend — no deep flexion involved.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Arms are passive — no shoulder stress.
proTip: Pretend there's a wall 6 inches behind you — reach your butt back to touch it.
contraindications: Inability to hip hinge without lumbar flexion

name: Hip Thrust
purpose: Maximum glute activation — higher than any squat or deadlift variation.
whyForYou: Builds the glutes your back desperately needs, with minimal spinal load.
steps: Upper back on bench edge. Feet flat, hip-width, directly under knees.
Drive through HEELS — squeeze glutes to lift hips to table-top.
HOLD 1s — hard glute squeeze. Chin tucked (look at knees, not ceiling).
Lower slowly — 2s descent.
coreBracing: MODERATE (40-50%) — posterior pelvic tilt at top.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: Chin TUCKED — looking at ceiling causes back extension. Posterior pelvic tilt at top protects spine. Start bodyweight only.
⚠️ KNEE: Feet directly under knees — not too far forward or back. Adjust until no knee discomfort.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Bench edge at mid-scapula. Pad if uncomfortable for labrum.
proTip: Start bodyweight. Add a dumbbell on hips when 15 reps is easy. Barbell comes much later.
contraindications: Inability to perform without feeling it in the back

name: Goblet Squat
purpose: Front-loaded squat that teaches upright torso — excellent squat pattern developer.
whyForYou: Front load counterbalances you upright — reduces back stress vs barbell squat. Teaches the pattern.
steps: Hold dumbbell/KB at chest with both hands — elbows under the weight.
Feet shoulder-width, toes slightly out. Brace core.
SIT DOWN AND BACK — 3s controlled descent to comfortable depth.
Drive through heels to stand. Stay upright throughout.
formCues: ✅ Chest stays tall — dumbbell keeps you upright
commonMistakes: ❌ Going too deep before earning the range
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: Front load reduces spinal shear vs back squat. Stay UPRIGHT. Light weight only in Phase 1.
⚠️ KNEE: Depth limited to comfort — don't force depth. VMO pre-activation mandatory. Watch for valgus on left side.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Holding DB at chest — if left shoulder can't hold position, reduce weight.
proTip: Use elbows to push knees out at the bottom — opens up the squat pattern.
contraindications: Inability to squat to comfortable depth without pain
Back safety level RESTRICTED or STOP

name: Box Squat
purpose: Controlled depth squat — box provides consistent depth target and safety net.
whyForYou: Controls depth precisely for your knee. The box catches you — removes fear of going too deep.
steps: Stand in front of box, feet shoulder-width. Hold dumbbells at sides (optional).
SIT BACK to box — 3s controlled descent. Shin stays vertical.
PAUSE 1s on box (don't collapse). Maintain tension.
Drive through heels to stand.
formCues: ✅ Stand up with glutes, not momentum
commonMistakes: ❌ Knees shooting forward over toes excessively
injuryNotes: ⚠️ KNEE: Box height controls depth — set it so left knee stays comfortable. Higher box = safer. VMO pre-activation mandatory.
⚠️ BACK: Sit BACK pattern reduces forward lean and spinal load. Keep chest tall.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Dumbbells at sides if used — no overhead loading.
proTip: Start with a high box (above parallel). Lower it 1 inch per week as mobility and strength improve.
contraindications: Inability to control descent to box

name: Step-Up
equipment: Sturdy box, bench, or step (6-12 inches)
purpose: Functional single-leg strength — mimics real-world movement (stairs, hiking).
whyForYou: Low box height is knee-friendly. Builds unilateral strength relevant to your sport interests (hiking, surfing).
steps: Place entire foot on box. Stand tall.
DRIVE through the heel of the top foot — step up fully. Don't push off back foot.
Stand tall on top. Controlled step down — 2s descent.
Complete all reps one leg, then switch.
formCues: ✅ Entire foot on box — heel included
✅ Knee tracks over toes — no valgus
injuryNotes: ⚠️ KNEE: Start with 6-inch box on left side. Increase height gradually. VMO pre-activation mandatory. Watch for valgus.
⚠️ BACK: Upright torso throughout — no forward lean. Hold wall/rack for balance if needed.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Holding dumbbells at sides is fine if shoulder tolerates.
proTip: Start with a low step (6 inches). Only increase height when you can do 12 reps with perfect knee tracking.
contraindications: Acute knee pain on affected side

name: Farmer's Walk
purpose: Loaded carry — builds grip, core stability, and total-body resilience under load.
whyForYou: Walking under load trains core stability in a functional way. One of the safest loaded exercises for your back.
steps: Pick up equal dumbbells/KBs — one in each hand.
Stand TALL. Shoulders back and down. Core braced.
Walk at controlled pace for 40s. Short steps, tall posture.
Set down with flat back — hinge, don't round.
formCues: ✅ Shoulders back and DOWN — no shrugging
✅ Walk tall — imagine balancing a book on your head
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: Surprisingly safe — vertical spine under symmetrical load. Start LIGHT. Set down with hip hinge, not rounded back.
⚠️ KNEE: Walk on flat surface. Short steps reduce knee impact.
⚠️ SHOULDER: If grip irritates shoulder, use lighter weight.
proTip: Pretend you're walking through a crowded room and don't want to bump anyone — forces perfect posture.
contraindications: Uneven walking surface with heavy load

name: Suitcase Carry
purpose: Anti-lateral flexion — trains core to resist side-bending under load.
whyForYou: Builds the lateral core stability your back needs. Walking + anti-flexion = functional rehab.
steps: Hold ONE dumbbell/KB in one hand at your side.
Stand TALL — do NOT lean toward or away from the weight.
Walk 30s. Body stays perfectly upright — as if carrying nothing.
Switch hands. Repeat.
commonMistakes: ❌ Weight too heavy — can't maintain upright posture
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: Excellent for lateral core stability. Start VERY light — if you lean at all, weight is too heavy.
⚠️ KNEE: Flat surface. Short steps.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Alternate which hand carries — don't always load the same side.
proTip: Check in a mirror — you should look identical to walking without a weight. If you can see the lean, go lighter.

name: Half-Kneeling Cable Chop
purpose: Diagonal core pattern in a stable base — rotational control without spinal risk.
whyForYou: Half-kneeling locks out lumbar rotation — all movement comes from core, not lower back.
steps: Half-kneeling perpendicular to cable. Inside knee down.
Grab rope/handle with both hands at shoulder height.
CHOP diagonally DOWN and ACROSS body. Rotate from torso, not arms.
Return slowly. Complete all reps, switch sides.
formCues: ✅ Hips stay SQUARE — don't rotate pelvis
✅ Movement from thoracic spine, not lumbar
✅ Arms are guides — core does the work
commonMistakes: ❌ Rotating from hips instead of thoracic
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: Half-kneeling locks pelvis — protects lumbar spine. Light weight. Rotation must come from THORACIC spine, not lower back.
⚠️ KNEE: Pad the kneeling knee. Ensure comfortable position.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Keep arms in front of body — no reaching behind.
proTip: Squeeze the glute of the down knee — locks your pelvis even more firmly.
contraindications: Inability to maintain half-kneeling without pain

name: Dead Bug with Band
equipment: Mat, resistance band or light dumbbell
purpose: Progressed dead bug — added resistance increases anti-extension demand.
whyForYou: Once bodyweight dead bug is mastered, this is the next step for your core rehab progression.
steps: Lie flat. Press back INTO floor. Hold band overhead (anchored) or hold light DB.
Arms up, knees at 90°. Band provides constant pull to extend you.
RESIST the pull. Extend opposite arm and leg — back stays GLUED to floor.
Exhale 4s. Pause 1s. Return. Switch sides.
formCues: ✅ Back NEVER leaves floor — the band makes this harder
✅ Slow and controlled — speed defeats the purpose
commonMistakes: ❌ Band too heavy — back lifts off
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: If lower back lifts AT ALL, remove the band and do regular dead bug. Progress only when bodyweight version is flawless.
⚠️ KNEE: No impact — knees stay at 90°.
⚠️ SHOULDER: If holding band overhead bothers labrum, hold light DB at chest instead.
proTip: Master 3×10 regular dead bug with ZERO back lift before adding the band.
contraindications: Cannot perform regular dead bug without back lifting

name: Band Lateral Walk
equipment: Mini loop band (around ankles or above knees)
purpose: Targets glute med — the #1 stabilizer for knee tracking and pelvic control.
whyForYou: Weak glute med = knee valgus = knee pain. This directly protects your left knee.
steps: Band around ankles (harder) or above knees (easier). Quarter squat position.
Step RIGHT — push through LEFT heel. Keep tension on band.
Step LEFT foot in — DON'T let band go slack.
12 steps right, then 12 steps left.
formCues: ✅ Toes point forward — no duck feet
injuryNotes: ⚠️ KNEE: This exercise PROTECTS your knee by strengthening glute med. Band above knees if ankle position bothers knee.
⚠️ BACK: Stay in quarter squat — no bending forward.
⚠️ SHOULDER: No impact.
proTip: Place hands on glutes while walking — you should feel them working with every step.

name: Side-Lying Hip Abduction
purpose: Isolates glute med — the most important muscle for knee and pelvic stability.
whyForYou: Direct glute med isolation for your left knee protection. Rehab staple.
steps: Lie on side. Bottom leg bent for stability. Top leg straight.
Lift top leg approximately 30-40° — lead with the HEEL, not the toe.
HOLD 1s at top. Lower slowly — don't drop.
Complete all reps, then switch sides.
formCues: ✅ Lead with HEEL — slight external rotation
✅ Body stays STACKED — no rolling back
commonMistakes: ❌ Rolling hips back (recruits hip flexors instead)
injuryNotes: ⚠️ KNEE: This exercise HELPS your knee — strengthens the muscle that prevents valgus.
⚠️ BACK: Keep body stacked. Place pillow under waist if side-lying bothers back.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Bottom arm under head for support — shouldn't stress shoulder.
proTip: Place hand on outer hip — you should feel the glute med fire. If you feel the front of the hip, you're rolling back.
contraindications: Acute hip bursitis on working side

name: Standing Calf Raise
purpose: Calf strength for ankle stability, walking, and sport performance.
whyForYou: Ankle stability supports your knee. Strong calves = better hiking, surfing, and daily function.
steps: Stand on edge of step — heels hanging off. Hold wall/rack for balance.
Rise onto toes — SQUEEZE calves at top. Hold 1s.
Lower slowly below the step — full stretch. 2s descent.
Control the entire range — no bouncing.
formCues: ✅ Full range of motion — below step to full rise
commonMistakes: ❌ Partial range — not getting full stretch
injuryNotes: ⚠️ KNEE: Straight knee targets gastrocnemius. If knee complains, do seated version (bent knee targets soleus).
⚠️ BACK: Hold support for balance — no twisting. Low spinal load.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Hold rail/wall with one hand — no overhead component.
proTip: Do single-leg to expose side-to-side imbalances. The weak side gets extra reps.

name: Dumbbell Bicep Curl
purpose: Elbow flexion isolation — supports pulling movements and grip strength.
whyForYou: Supports your rowing and deadlift grip. Light isolation work aids connective tissue health.
steps: Stand tall, dumbbells at sides, palms forward (supinated).
Curl both dumbbells up — keep elbows PINNED to sides.
Squeeze at top. Lower slowly — 2s descent. Full extension at bottom.
formCues: ✅ Elbows stay at your sides — don't drift forward
✅ Full extension at bottom — no partial reps
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: No swinging or leaning back — this causes back compression. Stand against wall if needed.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Neutral grip (hammer curl) if supinated grip bothers labrum.
⚠️ KNEE: No impact.
proTip: Stand against a wall — if your elbows or back leave the wall, you're using momentum.

name: Band Face Pull
equipment: Resistance band, door anchor or pole
purpose: Home/outdoor alternative to cable face pulls — same shoulder-protective benefit.
whyForYou: When you train at home or outdoors, you still need face pulls. This is how.
steps: Anchor band at face height (door anchor, pole, tree).
Grab band ends with overhand grip. Step back for tension.
Pull elbows BACK and APART — bring band to face. External rotate at end.
SQUEEZE 1s. Return slowly.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ SHOULDER: MANDATORY for home/outdoor days — same labrum protection as cable face pulls.
⚠️ BACK: Light movement — no spinal stress.
⚠️ KNEE: No impact.
proTip: Wrap band around a pole at face height — gives clearest line of pull for the movement.
contraindications: No secure anchor point for band

name: Lateral Lunge
purpose: Frontal plane movement — most training is sagittal. This fills the gap.
whyForYou: Lateral movement strength is critical for surfing, BJJ, and injury prevention.
steps: Stand feet together. Step RIGHT foot wide to the side.
SIT back and RIGHT — weight into right hip. Left leg stays straight.
Push through right foot to return to center.
Complete all reps one side, then switch.
formCues: ✅ Sit BACK into the hip — not forward
coreBracing: MODERATE (30-40%) — maintain balance in lateral plane.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ KNEE: Start with small steps — increase width as comfortable. LEFT side may need shallower depth. Watch for valgus.
⚠️ BACK: Keep chest tall — lateral movement shouldn't stress back. Bodyweight only in Phase 1.
⚠️ SHOULDER: No impact — arms at sides or holding light DB at chest.
proTip: Start bodyweight with small steps. Think 'sit into the hip' not 'lunge sideways.'

name: Band-Assisted Row
equipment: Resistance band, door anchor or sturdy anchor point
purpose: Home/outdoor rowing alternative — maintains pulling pattern without gym equipment.
whyForYou: When no gym is available, you still need to pull. Band rows keep the pattern alive.
steps: Anchor band at chest height. Grab both ends. Step back for tension.
Stand tall, slight lean back. Arms extended.
RETRACT shoulder blades. PULL elbows to ribs — squeeze 1s.
Return slowly — control the band forward.
commonMistakes: ❌ Band too light (no challenge at end range)
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: Standing row requires some spinal stabilization. If back is flared, sit on floor with legs extended and band around feet.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Neutral hand position — thumbs up. Don't force stretch at end range.
⚠️ KNEE: No impact.
proTip: Double the band or step further back to increase difficulty at end range.
contraindications: No secure anchor point for band
Acute lower back pain (switch to seated floor version)

name: Cable Woodchop Low-to-High
purpose: Rotational core power in a controlled setting — functional for sport and daily life.
whyForYou: Rotational strength for surfing, BJJ, and muay thai — your sports all demand this pattern.
steps: Stand perpendicular to LOW cable. Grab handle with both hands.
Rotate and lift handle diagonally from low (near hip) to HIGH (opposite shoulder).
Rotate from THORACIC spine — hips stay relatively stable.
Return slowly along the same path.
formCues: ✅ Pivot from mid-back — not lower back
✅ Arms are guides — core generates the rotation
commonMistakes: ❌ Rotating from lumbar spine (dangerous for you)
❌ Using arms to pull instead of core to rotate
coreBracing: HIGH (60-70%) — controlled rotation with spinal protection.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: ALL rotation must come from thoracic spine — NOT lumbar. Light weight. If any back pain, STOP and switch to Pallof Press.
⚠️ KNEE: Feet stay planted — no pivoting on toes.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Keep hands in front of body at all times — no reaching behind.
proTip: Film yourself from behind — if your lower back is twisting visibly, the weight is too heavy or you're initiating wrong.
contraindications: Inability to differentiate thoracic from lumbar rotation
Phase 1 (master Pallof Press and half-kneeling chop first)

name: 90/90 Hip Switch
purpose: Restores internal and external hip rotation — both are essential for pain-free movement.
whyForYou: Tight hips = back compensates for missing hip ROM = back pain. This breaks that cycle.
steps: Sit on floor, both knees bent at 90°. Feet wide.
Let both knees fall to the RIGHT — hold one breath cycle.
SWITCH — let both knees fall to the LEFT.
Move smoothly between sides — one breath per switch.
formCues: ✅ Move from hips, not just knees
commonMistakes: ❌ Forcing range — respect what your hips give you today
injuryNotes: ⚠️ KNEE: Widen feet if left knee is uncomfortable. Never force the knee to the ground.
⚠️ BACK: Sit on a yoga block or pillow if you can't sit tall without rounding.
⚠️ SHOULDER: No impact.
proTip: Do 2 minutes of this every single morning — your hips and back will transform in weeks.
contraindications: Acute hip labral tear with clicking
Acute knee injury (skip the affected side)

name: Child's Pose + Lat Reach
purpose: Decompresses spine after loaded exercises. Opens lats and lower back.
whyForYou: Reverses compression from deadlifts and rows. Your back's 'reset button.'
steps: Kneel on mat. Sit hips BACK toward heels. Walk hands forward.
Walk hands to the RIGHT — hold 30s. Breathe into the left side.
Walk hands to the LEFT — hold 30s. Breathe into the right side.
Return to CENTER — hold 30s. Exhale longer than inhale.
formCues: ✅ Hips sit BACK — don't hover forward
✅ Exhale longer than inhale (4 in, 6 out)
coreBracing: ZERO — fully relaxed. Let spine decompress.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: One of the BEST positions for your post-surgical back. Daily if possible.
⚠️ KNEE: If kneeling is uncomfortable, place rolled towel behind knees.
⚠️ SHOULDER: If left shoulder limits the reach, place a yoga block under that hand.
proTip: This is your 'reset button' — use it any time your back feels compressed during the day.
contraindications: Acute knee injury preventing kneeling (do seated forward fold instead)

name: Hamstring Stretch
purpose: Tight hamstrings tilt pelvis posteriorly, increasing lower back pressure.
whyForYou: Biggest modifiable contributor to your post-surgical back pain. Must stretch daily.
steps: Lie flat on back. Loop strap around one foot.
Lift leg toward ceiling — pull gently to 6/10 stretch. NOT 10/10.
Hold 30s. Breathe — exhale into the stretch.
Switch legs. Repeat.
formCues: ✅ Knee stays straight (slight bend is OK)
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: If you feel tingling, numbness, or radiating pain down the leg = RELEASE IMMEDIATELY. This could be nerve, not muscle.
⚠️ KNEE: Slight knee bend is fine if hamstring is very tight.
⚠️ SHOULDER: No impact.
proTip: Flex the foot (toes toward you) for a nerve glide component — helps mobilize the sciatic nerve.
contraindications: Radiating pain down the leg (nerve issue — see PT)

name: Pigeon Stretch
tempo: Static hold — sink deeper with each exhale
purpose: Opens deep external hip rotators — tight piriformis can mimic sciatica.
whyForYou: Tight piriformis compresses the sciatic nerve — which your post-surgical back can't afford.
steps: From all fours, slide RIGHT knee forward behind right wrist.
Extend LEFT leg straight back. Hips square to the ground.
Walk hands forward — fold over front leg as tolerated.
Hold 45s. Breathe deeply into the stretch. Switch sides.
formCues: ✅ Hips stay SQUARE — don't let them rotate
✅ Front shin angle based on flexibility (more parallel = harder)
✅ Exhale to sink deeper — no forcing
commonMistakes: ❌ Forcing front shin parallel too soon
injuryNotes: ⚠️ KNEE: LEFT knee — if front-leg position causes knee pain, do Figure-4 Stretch (c7) instead. The knee should feel zero stress.
⚠️ BACK: Fold forward only as far as back allows. Place block under front hip if it doesn't reach the floor.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Use blocks under hands if reaching floor is difficult.
proTip: If your hip doesn't reach the floor, put a yoga block or folded towel under it. Keeps hips level.
contraindications: Acute knee pain in the pigeon position (use Figure-4 instead)

name: Doorway Pec Stretch
purpose: Opens chest and anterior shoulder — counteracts forward posture from daily life.
whyForYou: Tight pecs pull shoulder forward, stressing the labrum. This opens that up safely.
steps: Stand in doorway. Place forearm on doorframe at LOW angle (45° below shoulder).
Step through with one foot — feel stretch across chest. Hold 30s.
Move arm to MID angle (shoulder height). Hold 30s.
Move arm to HIGH angle (45° above shoulder — ONLY if pain-free). Hold 30s.
commonMistakes: ❌ Forcing the high angle (dangerous for labrum)
❌ Rotating torso instead of stepping through
injuryNotes: ⚠️ SHOULDER: SKIP the HIGH angle (above shoulder) if it causes ANY pinching or discomfort in left shoulder. Low and mid angles only until labrum improves.
⚠️ BACK: Stand tall through the stretch — no arching back to compensate.
⚠️ KNEE: No impact.
proTip: The low angle is often the tightest — spend extra time there. That's pec minor, which most people neglect.
contraindications: Acute anterior shoulder pain (skip high angle)

name: Side-Lying Quad Stretch
purpose: Stretches quads and hip flexors — tight hip flexors tilt pelvis anteriorly, stressing lower back.
whyForYou: Tight quads/hip flexors pull your pelvis forward and compress your lower back. Must address daily.
steps: Lie on your side. Bottom leg slightly bent for balance.
Grab top ankle (or use strap). Pull heel toward glute.
Keep knees together. Push hip slightly forward for deeper stretch.
Hold 30s. Breathe deeply. Switch sides.
formCues: ✅ Hip pushes slightly forward — stretches hip flexor too
injuryNotes: ⚠️ KNEE: LEFT side — use strap if you can't reach ankle comfortably. DO NOT force deep flexion. Pull only to mild stretch — no sharp knee pain.
⚠️ BACK: Keep pelvis neutral — no excessive arching. If back hurts, reduce pull.
⚠️ SHOULDER: If reaching back bothers left shoulder, use strap or towel to extend reach.
proTip: Side-lying version is safer for your back than standing — gravity doesn't pull you into extension.

name: Figure-4 Stretch
purpose: Supine pigeon alternative — same deep hip stretch, easier on the knee.
whyForYou: Knee-friendly alternative to pigeon stretch. Same piriformis release, zero knee stress.
steps: Lie on back. Cross RIGHT ankle over LEFT knee (making a '4' shape).
Pull LEFT thigh toward chest — feel stretch in RIGHT glute/hip.
Hold 30s. Breathe into the stretch.
Switch sides.
formCues: ✅ Flexed foot on the crossed leg (protects knee)
injuryNotes: ⚠️ KNEE: Flex the foot of the crossed leg — protects knee ligaments. If left knee can't tolerate crossing, do seated version on a chair.
⚠️ BACK: Supine position — back is supported. One of the safest hip stretches for you.
⚠️ SHOULDER: If reaching behind thigh is hard, use a strap/towel.
proTip: Press the crossed knee gently away with your elbow for a deeper stretch into external rotation.
contraindications: Acute knee injury preventing crossing position

name: Thread the Needle
purpose: Thoracic rotation stretch — opens the mid-back and posterior shoulder.
whyForYou: Opens your thoracic spine so it can rotate freely — keeps rotation demand OFF your lumbar spine.
steps: Start on all fours. Slide RIGHT arm UNDER the left arm (threading).
Let right shoulder and temple rest on the floor.
Hold 2 breaths. Then open — reach right arm to ceiling.
Return to thread. Repeat 8 times, then switch sides.
formCues: ✅ Hips stay stacked over knees throughout
✅ Reach through fully — feel the mid-back open
✅ Open to ceiling fully — feel the chest open
commonMistakes: ❌ Not reaching far enough in either direction
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: Excellent for thoracic mobility — reduces lumbar rotation demand. Keep hips stable.
⚠️ KNEE: Pad knees if uncomfortable on all fours.
⚠️ SHOULDER: If left shoulder catches during the thread, reduce depth. The open-to-ceiling phase may need to be limited.
proTip: Breathe OUT as you thread — you'll slide deeper. Breathe IN as you open — you'll reach further.
contraindications: Acute shoulder dislocation on threading side

name: Supine Spinal Twist
purpose: Gentle spinal rotation with full back support — decompresses and realigns.
whyForYou: Supported spinal rotation — the floor does the work. Safe end-of-session release for your back.
steps: Lie flat on back. Bring knees to chest.
Let both knees fall to the RIGHT. Keep LEFT shoulder on the floor.
Arms out in T-position. Look LEFT (opposite to knees).
Hold 30s. Breathe deeply — exhale to sink deeper. Switch sides.
formCues: ✅ Let gravity pull knees — no forcing
coreBracing: ZERO — completely passive. Let gravity do everything.
injuryNotes: ⚠️ BACK: Gentle and supported — safe for post-surgical back. If any sharp pain, reduce the twist (don't let knees go as far). STOP if radiating symptoms.
⚠️ KNEE: Place pillow between knees for comfort.
⚠️ SHOULDER: T-position arms — if left shoulder doesn't reach the floor, bend the elbow. No forcing.
proTip: This is your last exercise of the session — take your time. 5 slow breaths each side minimum.
contraindications: Acute disc herniation with rotational intolerance
Radiating nerve pain during the twist

name: Standing Calf Stretch
purpose: Restores calf/ankle flexibility — tight calves limit squat depth and increase knee stress.
whyForYou: Tight calves restrict ankle dorsiflexion — which causes compensations up the chain to your knee and back.
steps: Face wall. Step one foot back — keep back heel DOWN.
Lean into wall — feel stretch in back calf. Leg stays straight.
Hold 30s. Then BEND the back knee slightly — stretches soleus.
Hold 15s bent. Switch legs.
formCues: ✅ Back heel stays ON the ground
✅ Toes point straight ahead — no duck foot
✅ Two phases: straight leg (gastroc) then bent (soleus)
injuryNotes: ⚠️ KNEE: Bent-knee phase should be gentle — stop if knee discomfort.
⚠️ BACK: Lean forward from the ankle, not by rounding back.
⚠️ SHOULDER: Just leaning on wall — no stress.
proTip: Do both straight-leg AND bent-knee versions — they hit different muscles. Most people skip the bent-knee.
